Madagascar Influenza — Phylogenetic Analysis

IQ-TREE ML trees · Monophyletic clade analysis

Author

Camila Duitama

Published

April 23, 2026

← Back to main report

1 Pipeline

10 maximum-likelihood trees have been built across HA and NA segments for all subtypes with ≥ 10 Madagascar and ≥ 20 other African sequences. All sequences are used — no subsampling.

Category Segments Strategy Trees
HA per-subtype HA One tree per HA subtype 5
NA per-subtype NA One tree per NA subtype 5
Total (current) 10

Tree naming: each tree is named <segment>_<subtype>. For example, HA_B means the Hemagglutinin (HA) gene segment of Influenza B viruses; NA_H3N2 means the Neuraminidase (NA) gene segment of H3N2 viruses. HA and NA are the two surface proteins that determine subtype (the H and N numbers) and are the main targets of the immune response.

1.1 IQ-TREE parameters

Each tree was built with IQ-TREE 3.1.0 using the following command:

iqtree3 -s <alignment.fasta> \
        -m HKY+G          \ # substitution model: HKY (transitions ≠ transversions) + Gamma rate variation
        -B 1000           \ # ultrafast bootstrap (UFBoot2): 1000 replicates, values shown as node labels
        -T <N_CPUs>       \ # multi-threading
        --seed 42         \ # reproducibility
        --prefix <NAME>   \ # output prefix
        --redo               # overwrite existing run
Parameter Value Meaning
-m HKY+G HKY + Gamma 2-parameter nucleotide model (κ for ts/tv ratio) with 4 discrete rate categories
-B 1000 UFBoot2 Ultrafast bootstrap approximation; node support values are bootstrap percentages
--seed 42 42 Fixed random seed for reproducibility

Branch support: node labels are UFBoot2 percentages (0–100). Values ≥ 95 are considered strongly supported.

No subsampling: all Africa sequences (Madagascar + rest of Africa) with the relevant segment enter the alignment directly. IQ-TREE internally deduplicates identical sequences for the ML optimisation, but adds them back as tips in the final tree — so every input sequence appears in the treefile.

1.2 Tree rooting

IQ-TREE outputs unrooted trees (no -o outgroup was specified). Before any clade detection, each tree is midpoint-rooted: the root is placed at the midpoint of the longest tip-to-tip path in the tree. This is implemented via ete3’s get_midpoint_outgroup() / set_outgroup() and is a standard approach when no outgroup sequence is available.

1.3 Clade detection

Before looking for Madagascar-only groups, every tree branch with UFBoot2 support < 70 is collapsed.

A monophyletic Madagascar clade is then defined as the largest group of tree tips (≥ 2) that are all exclusively from Madagascar and share a common ancestor not shared with any non-Madagascar sequence.

2 Statistics computed per tree

Metric Meaning
n_mdg_clades Number of monophyletic Madagascar clades (≥ 2 tips)
mdg_pd_fraction What fraction of the tree’s total branch length is “owned” by Madagascar sequences (see below)
mrca_subtree_fraction How much of the tree sits inside the subtree that spans all Madagascar sequences (see below)

mdg_pd_fraction — how much of the tree’s evolutionary space do Madagascar sequences cover?

Imagine the tree as a road map: every branch is a road segment with a length equal to the number of mutations along that branch. Faith’s Phylogenetic Diversity (PD) for Madagascar is the total length of all roads you need to drive to visit every Madagascar sequence starting from the root. If Madagascar sequences are scattered all over the tree, you need to drive almost the entire map — high PD fraction. If they all cluster in one corner, you only need a short detour — low PD fraction. This metric is divided by the total map length so it becomes a percentage of the whole tree.

mrca_subtree_fraction — how spread out are Madagascar sequences across the tree?

The MRCA (Most Recent Common Ancestor) of all Madagascar sequences is the single node in the tree that is the common ancestor of every Madagascar tip. It is essentially the point where all Madagascar lineages diverge. The subtree below that node contains everything nested between Madagascar sequences — including non-Madagascar sequences that happen to sit among them. mrca_subtree_fraction is the branch-length sum of that whole subtree divided by the total tree length.

  • Value ≈ 1.0 (e.g., Flu B, H3N2): The MRCA is near the root, meaning Madagascar sequences span almost the entire tree. This happens when Madagascar has sequences in many different global lineages — consistent with frequent re-importation every season.
  • Value ≈ 0.03 (e.g., NA_H9N2): The MRCA subtree covers only 3% of the tree. All Madagascar sequences are bunched together in a tiny corner — consistent with a single introduction that stayed isolated.

3 Statistics computed per clade

Metric Meaning
internal_bl Sum of branch lengths within the clade — within-Madagascar diversity
stem_bl Branch from clade root to parent — divergence from nearest African ancestor
clade_prop_tree_bl internal_bl ÷ total tree branch length — clade’s share of total tree diversity
Note

Results are based on 10 maximum-likelihood trees (5 HA + 5 NA, full Africa sequences, no subsampling, UFBoot2 1000 replicates).

4 Input data coverage

For each tree, every Africa sequence with the relevant segment was used — no subsampling. IQ-TREE removes sequences that are 100% identical during the ML tree search (to save computation), but adds them back as tips in the final tree — so the treefile contains all input sequences and no sequence is truly excluded. The difference between GISAID isolate counts and tree tips reflects isolates that exist in GISAID but for which the relevant gene segment (HA or NA) was never sequenced or was not included in the GISAID download.

¹ Total GISAID Africa isolates with this subtype (all hosts, from combined_metadata.tsv).
² Isolates in GISAID that do not have the relevant gene segment (HA or NA) sequenced; these were not part of any tree.

Trees range from 64 (HA_H6N2) to 9,587 (HA_H3N2) sequences; Madagascar sequences account for 2.6–43.1% of each tree.

5 Clade analysis results

5.1 Summary per tree

Across the 10 trees, Madagascar sequences form 22.2 ± 19.5 clades per tree on average, grouping 79% of sequences; MRCA fraction ranges from 0.027 (NA_H9N2) to 1.000 (HA_B), spanning tight local clustering to broad seasonal spread.

5.2 Top clades per tree

HA_H3N2 has the largest top clades on average (46 Madagascar sequences per clade); across all trees the five largest clades contain 20.9 ± 18.9 sequences on average.

6 Tree visualisation

6.1 Subsampling for visualisation

Full trees can contain thousands of sequences, making them impossible to read visually. To produce viewable trees, we subsampled each tree keeping all Madagascar sequences and selecting up to 150 non-Madagascar sequences using Treemmer (Menardo et al. 2018, BMC Bioinformatics 19:164).

Each tree was reduced using the following command:

python Treemmer_v0.3.py <tree.nwk> -X <target> -lm protected.csv -mc <n_mdg> -np -c 4

where -X sets the target number of tips, -lm protected.csv is a file listing all Madagascar tip names with the tag Madagascar, and -mc <n_mdg> forces Treemmer to always keep at least that many Madagascar-tagged sequences (equal to the total number of Madagascar tips, so none can be removed). All 10 subsampled trees were annotated with tip colours (Madagascar = red, other Africa = grey) and readable tip labels (Madagascar sequences only) for upload to iTOL.

The table below summarises the composition of each subsampled tree:

Tree Original tips Madagascar tips Non-Madagascar tips kept Total after subsampling
HA_B 5,015 365 150 515
HA_H1N1 8,621 234 150 384
HA_H3N2 9,587 375 150 525
HA_H6N2 64 27 37 64 (full tree, below target)
HA_H9N2 1,268 51 150 201
NA_B 4,292 258 150 408
NA_H1N1 7,186 203 150 353
NA_H3N2 8,114 369 150 519
NA_H6N2 65 28 37 65 (full tree, below target)
NA_H9N2 1,456 53 150 203

6.2 ete3 circular plots

Trees are plotted with ete3 in circular mode. Branch lengths are log-transformed (log(1 + d/m) where m is the median non-zero branch length across all branches) before rendering so that both short and long branches are visible in the same image — the absolute scale is not meaningful, only the relative spread matters. Madagascar sequences are shown in red; other Africa in grey. All trees are midpoint-rooted.

For each tree, two versions are shown: the subsampled tree (all Madagascar + up to 150 other Africa, used for iTOL upload) and the full tree (all Africa sequences used for IQ-TREE). HA_H6N2 and NA_H6N2 had fewer tips than the subsample target, so their subsampled and full trees are identical.

6.2.1 HA_H6N2

6.2.2 HA_B (Influenza B)

6.2.3 HA_H9N2

6.2.4 NA_H6N2
